name: Fetch, Summarize, and Report Website Links
do_pattern:
web_fetch(url=<main_url>)
extract links from HTML (up to 10)
FOR EACH link:
  content = web_fetch(url=link)
  summary = extract medium-length summary (3-5 sentences)
  write(path="summaries/<link_slug>.md", content=summary)
read all summary files
generate final synthesis across all summaries
write(path="<output_report>.md", content=report_with_sections)
dont_pattern:
DON'T fetch all 10 links into context at once
DON'T store all summaries in memory before writing
DON'T skip writing intermediate summaries to disk
DON'T include external domains without filtering
DON'T attempt to summarize without extracting key sentences first
trigger_description: When you need to analyze a website and its linked pages, extract key insights from each, and produce a consolidated markdown report.
reasoning: Sequential fetch-summarize-write leverages Captain Claw's scale loop to trim fetched content from context after each write, keeping memory constant regardless of link count. Writing summaries immediately prevents context explosion and enables the final synthesis step to work with manageable summary files rather than raw page content.
tags: web-research, batch-processing, link-extraction, summarization, report-generation
examples:
web_fetch(url="https://example.com")
# Extract links: [link1, link2, ..., link10]
FOR link in links:
  page = web_fetch(url=link)
  # Extract 3-5 key sentences
  summary = "Key finding 1. Key finding 2. Key finding 3."
  write(path="summaries/link_1.md", content=summary)
# After loop, read summaries/ and synthesize
write(path="report.md", content="# Website Analysis Report

## Link 1
<summary>

## Link 2
<summary>

## Synthesis
<combined_insights>")

name: Data Quality Audit with Remediation Plan
do_pattern:
1. Ingest data: glob() for local files, extract via pdf_extract/xlsx_extract/csv read, query databases via direct_api or shell
2. Profile each column: cardinality, null %, unique %, data type, format patterns (regex)
3. Detect anomalies: outliers (IQR), format violations, unexpected nulls, duplicates
4. Validate foreign keys: FOR EACH relationship, query both tables, find orphaned/missing refs
5. Score each field: impact = (% affected rows) × (business criticality) × (fix complexity)
6. Generate HTML report: embed profiling tables, distribution charts (image_gen), FK violation summaries, ranked remediation list
7. write() final report to output file
dont_pattern:
1. DON'T load all data into memory at once — use summarize_files or per-file extraction
2. DON'T skip null/format checks — they're often the largest quality issues
3. DON'T validate FKs by fetching entire tables into context — use database queries or filtered reads
4. DON'T produce a flat list of issues — prioritize by impact and fix effort
5. DON'T omit visualizations — distributions and missing-data patterns are hard to spot in tables
6. DON'T assume schema — always infer or ask for data dictionary upfront
trigger_description: When you need to assess data quality across mixed sources (files + databases), profile columns, detect anomalies, validate foreign keys, and produce a prioritized remediation roadmap.
reasoning: Data quality audits require systematic profiling, anomaly detection, and relationship validation to identify root causes and prioritize fixes. Embedding visualizations in the report makes patterns visible and remediation priorities defensible.
tags: data-quality, profiling, anomaly-detection, foreign-key-validation, audit, remediation, html-report
examples:
# Extract and profile CSVs
files = glob(pattern="<data_dir>/**/*.csv")
FOR EACH file:
  data = read(path=file)
  profile = {null_pct, cardinality, format_violations, outliers}
  write(path="profiles/<filename>_profile.json", content=profile)

# Validate foreign keys
FOR EACH (parent_table, child_table, fk_column):
  orphaned = query("SELECT * FROM child WHERE fk_column NOT IN (SELECT id FROM parent)")
  write(path="fk_violations/<relationship>.csv", content=orphaned)

# Generate HTML report with embedded charts
scores = rank_fields_by_impact(profiles, fk_violations)
charts = image_gen(prompt="bar chart: data quality score by field")
html = render_template(scores=scores, charts=charts, violations=fk_violations)
write(path="data_quality_report.html", content=html)

name: Meeting Preparation Brief Generator
do_pattern:
1. Ask user: meeting topic, attendee list, desired depth (quick or comprehensive)
2. google_mail(search="from:<attendee> OR to:<attendee>", max_results=30, date_range="last 30 days") for each attendee
3. google_calendar(list_events=true, attendees=<attendee_list>, date_range="last 90 days") to find past meetings
4. google_drive(search="<topic> OR <attendee_names>", max_results=20, date_range="last 30 days") for shared docs
5. web_search(query="<topic> <attendee_names> OR <company_names>") for external context
6. FOR EACH result set: extract key info, write to temp file
7. Aggregate all findings: attendee profiles, thread summaries with links, doc list with links, suggested agenda items, talking points
8. write(path="meeting_prep_<topic>_<date>.md", content=formatted_brief)
dont_pattern:
1. DON'T fetch all Gmail threads into context at once — use max_results limit and date filtering
2. DON'T process attendees in parallel without aggregation strategy — process sequentially and write summaries immediately
3. DON'T include full email body text — extract key points and link to thread
4. DON'T ignore date filters — always limit to last 30 days for emails/docs, 90 days for calendar
5. DON'T skip web search — external context often reveals industry news or competitor moves relevant to meeting
6. DON'T ask user to manually specify depth after starting — ask upfront to shape search scope
trigger_description: When preparing for an upcoming meeting, gather attendee context, recent communications, shared documents, and external research to produce a comprehensive prep brief.
reasoning: Meeting prep requires synthesizing multiple data sources (email, calendar, docs, web) efficiently without overwhelming context. Sequential processing with immediate writes leverages the scale loop to handle large attendee lists and result sets. Date filtering and result limits prevent token explosion while capturing recent, relevant context.
tags: meeting-prep, email, calendar, drive, web-research, aggregation, brief-generation
examples:
google_mail(search="from:alice@company.com OR to:alice@company.com", max_results=30, date_range="last 30 days")
google_calendar(list_events=true, attendees=["alice@company.com", "bob@company.com"], date_range="last 90 days")
google_drive(search="Q4 planning alice bob", max_results=20, date_range="last 30 days")
web_search(query="acme corp Q4 strategy market trends")
FOR EACH thread/doc/event: extract summary → write(path="prep_temp/<slug>.md", content=summary)
read all prep_temp files → aggregate → write final brief

name: Competitive Feature Analysis & Gap Report
do_pattern:
1. read(path=<feature_file>) to load your product features
2. web_search(query="top competitors in <market/category>") to identify top 10 competitors
3. FOR EACH competitor (top 10):
   - web_search(query="<competitor_name> product features pricing")
   - web_fetch(url=top_result.url) to get main product page
   - extract features, pricing tiers, feature availability
   - write(path="competitors/<competitor_name>.md", content=extracted_data)
4. read all competitor markdown files
5. build comparison matrix: rows=all_features, cols=[your_product, competitor1, ..., competitor10]
6. write(path="comparison_matrix.md", content=markdown_table)
7. write(path="comparison_matrix.csv", content=csv_format)
8. analyze gaps: features competitors have that you don't, features you have that competitors don't
9. write(path="competitive_analysis_report.md", content=gaps + advantages + strategic_recommendations)
dont_pattern:
1. DON'T fetch all competitor pages into context at once — fetch one at a time and write immediately
2. DON'T search for competitors and product pages in a single query — separate: market search first, then per-competitor product search
3. DON'T manually build the matrix in conversation — write to files (markdown + CSV) for reusability
4. DON'T include raw HTML or unstructured web content in the matrix — extract and normalize feature names first
5. DON'T skip pricing tier analysis — note which features are in which tiers
6. DON'T produce only a report — output both human-readable (markdown) and machine-readable (CSV) formats
trigger_description: When you need to analyze your product's features against top competitors, identify market gaps, and generate strategic recommendations based on competitive positioning.
reasoning: Competitive analysis requires systematic web research across multiple sources without exploding context. Fetching one competitor at a time with immediate file writes leverages the scale loop's context trimming. Dual output formats (markdown + CSV) enable both human review and downstream data analysis.
tags: competitive-analysis, market-research, feature-comparison, gap-analysis, web-research, batch-processing
examples:
web_search(query="top 10 SaaS project management competitors 2024")
web_search(query="Asana product features pricing")
web_fetch(url="https://asana.com/product")
# extract: features=[task management, timeline, portfolios, ...], pricing_tiers=[free, premium, business]
write(path="competitors/asana.md", content="## Asana\n\n### Features\n- Task management\n- Timeline view\n\n### Pricing\nFree, Premium ($10.99/user/mo), Business ($24.99/user/mo)")

# After all competitors fetched and written:
read(path="competitors/asana.md")
read(path="competitors/monday.md")
# ... build matrix
write(path="comparison_matrix.md", content="| Feature | Your Product | Asana | Monday | ... |\n|---------|---|---|---|\n| Task Management | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |")
write(path="comparison_matrix.csv", content="Feature,Your Product,Asana,Monday,...")

name: Organize Downloads Folder by Type, Date, Size
do_pattern:
files = glob(pattern="<input_dir>/**/*")
FOR EACH file:
  type_cat = categorize_by_extension(file) → documents|images|videos|archives|code|data|unknown
  date_cat = categorize_by_mtime(file) → this-week|this-month|older
  size_cat = categorize_by_size(file) → large|medium|small
  dest_path = "<output_dir>/<type_cat>/<date_cat>/<size_cat>/<filename>"
  IF file exists at dest_path:
    dest_path = append_timestamp(dest_path)
    log("RENAMED", file, dest_path)
  shell("mkdir -p <parent_of_dest>")
  shell("mv '<file>' '<dest_path>'")
  log("MOVED", file, dest_path)
write(path="<output_dir>/organization_report.md", content=detailed_log)
trigger_description: When a downloads or inbox folder becomes cluttered and needs systematic organization by file type, age, and size into a nested directory structure.
reasoning: Nested categorization with collision handling and detailed logging ensures files are systematically organized without data loss, and the report provides full audit trail for verification and recovery.
tags: file-management, batch-processing, organization, nested-structure, collision-handling, audit-logging
examples:
# Categorization logic (pseudo)
type_cat = {
  .pdf, .docx, .txt, .xlsx → documents
  .jpg, .png, .gif, .webp → images
  .mp4, .mkv, .mov → videos
  .zip, .tar, .gz, .rar → archives
  .py, .js, .go, .rs → code
  .csv, .json, .parquet → data
}

date_cat = {
  mtime >= today - 7 days → this-week
  mtime >= today - 30 days → this-month
  mtime < today - 30 days → older
}

size_cat = {
  size > 100MB → large
  10MB ≤ size ≤ 100MB → medium
  size < 10MB → small
}

# Report entry example:
MOVED: /home/user/Downloads/report.pdf → /organized/documents/this-week/small/report.pdf
RENAMED (collision): /home/user/Downloads/photo.jpg → /organized/images/this-month/medium/photo_2025-01-15T143022Z.jpg

name: Content Publishing Workflow
do_pattern:
read(path=<draft_file>)
use LLM to check spelling/grammar, return corrected content
links = extract all URLs from content
FOR EACH link:
  result = web_fetch(url=link)
  if result.status != 200: log to broken_links.md
  # scale loop trims fetched content
images = glob(pattern=<draft_dir>/**/*.{jpg,png,gif})
FOR EACH image:
  shell(cmd="imagemagick or similar to compress & resize")
  write optimized image to publish/images/
use LLM to extract marked snippets OR generate:
  - tweet-length (280 chars)
  - LinkedIn-length (1300 chars)
use LLM to adapt content into email newsletter format
write(path=publish/corrected_draft.md, content=corrected)
write(path=publish/social_snippets.json, content=snippets)
write(path=publish/newsletter.md, content=email_version)
write(path=publish/link_report.md, content=broken_links)
dont_pattern:
DON'T fetch all links into memory before processing — fetch one at a time, log result, discard
DON'T load all images into context — process and write each to disk immediately
DON'T generate snippets without checking for marked sections first
DON'T halt on broken links — flag and continue (unless explicitly critical)
DON'T store all outputs in memory — write each variant to its own file as generated
DON'T skip image optimization if images exist — always compress and resize
DON'T mix output formats — keep social snippets in JSON, newsletter in Markdown, reports in Markdown
trigger_description: When a draft document is ready for multi-channel publication and requires spelling/grammar check, link verification, image optimization, and social/email variants.
reasoning: Publishing requires parallel quality checks (spelling, links, images) and format variants (social, email) — this pattern processes each sequentially with immediate file writes to avoid context bloat and enable the scale loop to trim intermediate results.
tags: content-publishing, document-processing, link-verification, image-optimization, social-media, email, batch-output
examples:
# Extract and verify links
links = extract_urls(content)
FOR link in links:
  try: web_fetch(url=link, timeout=10s)
  catch timeout/404: append to broken_links.md

# Optimize images
shell(cmd="convert input.jpg -resize 1200x630 -quality 85 output.jpg")

# Generate social snippets
snippets = {
  "tweet": LLM(prompt="Summarize in 280 chars: <content>"),
  "linkedin": LLM(prompt="Professional summary in 1300 chars: <content>")
}
write(path="publish/social_snippets.json", content=json.dumps(snippets))

# Email newsletter
newsletter = LLM(prompt="Format as email newsletter with greeting, body, CTA: <content>")
write(path="publish/newsletter.md", content=newsletter)

name: Daily Standup Briefing with Auto-Priority
do_pattern:
1. google_calendar(list events for today)
2. google_mail(search unread messages, last 30 days for sender analysis)
3. todo(list all items, filter overdue + due today)
4. google_drive(list recently shared docs with me)
5. datastore(query sender_urgency_scores from past 30 days)
6. FOR EACH unread message: calculate sender_score = (reply_speed_percentile * 0.5) + (frequency_percentile * 0.5)
7. Rank items: meetings by time → overdue tasks → messages by sender_score → docs by share_date
8. write(path=briefing.md, content=formatted schedule + pending + messages + docs + suggested actions)
9. insights(store briefing_generated_at timestamp for cron scheduling)
dont_pattern:
1. DON'T fetch all Gmail threads into context — search unread only, extract sender + subject
2. DON'T hardcode sender priorities — always compute from historical data
3. DON'T list items in arbitrary order — always rank by auto-priority logic
4. DON'T ignore meeting conflicts — flag when overdue task due during back-to-back meetings
5. DON'T include full email bodies in briefing — summarize to 1-2 lines per message
6. DON'T forget to update sender_urgency_scores after each run — store reply patterns for next day
trigger_description: Every morning, compile today's schedule, pending tasks, unread messages, and shared docs into a prioritized briefing based on sender urgency and task deadlines.
reasoning: Auto-prioritizing by learned sender urgency (reply speed + frequency) reduces decision fatigue and surfaces the most time-sensitive communications first, while time-based ranking of tasks prevents missed deadlines during busy days.
tags: daily-standup, calendar, email, todo, auto-priority, briefing, morning-routine
examples:
# Example briefing output:

## Today's Schedule
- 09:00 AM: Team standup (30 min)
- 11:00 AM: 1:1 with manager (30 min)
- 02:00 PM: Project review (1 hour)

## Pending Items (Auto-Prioritized)
1. [URGENT] Reply to alice@company.com re: Q4 roadmap (sender_score: 0.92, unread 2h)
2. [OVERDUE] Finish design doc (due yesterday, conflicts with 11am meeting)
3. [DUE TODAY] Code review for PR #234 (due 5pm)
4. [INFO] New shared doc: 2024-budget.xlsx (shared 1h ago by finance team)

## Suggested Actions
- Reply to alice before 11am meeting
- Schedule 30min for code review after 2pm meeting
- Skim budget doc during lunch

name: Data-Driven Chart Generation & Insights Report
do_pattern:
1. glob(pattern='<input_dir>/**/*.{csv,json}') to find all data files
2. FOR EACH file:
   a. read(path=file) and load into pandas DataFrame
   b. shell('python -c "import pandas as pd; df=pd.read_csv/json(...); print(df.describe()); print(df.corr())"') for automated stats
   c. Pass raw data + stats to LLM: 'Identify 3-5 interesting patterns, trends, outliers, or correlations'
   d. For each pattern identified, generate a matplotlib/plotly Python script
   e. write(path='<scripts_dir>/<pattern_name>.py', content=script)
   f. shell('cd <scripts_dir> && python <pattern_name>.py') to produce <pattern_name>.png
   g. image_vision(path='<pattern_name>.png') to extract visual description
   h. write(path='<output_dir>/<pattern_name>_insight.md', content=description + key_findings)
3. Collect all insight files and images
4. Generate HTML with base64-encoded images: FOR EACH insight, embed image as data URI + text
5. write(path='<output_dir>/report.html', content=final_html)
dont_pattern:
1. DON'T load all data files into memory at once — process one file at a time
2. DON'T run all visualization scripts in parallel without error handling — run sequentially with try-catch per script
3. DON'T skip failed visualizations silently — log errors and attempt retry (pip install missing deps, adjust dtypes) before skipping
4. DON'T embed raw image file paths in HTML — always base64-encode images for self-contained output
5. DON'T generate visualizations without LLM analysis — let the agent identify what's interesting first
6. DON'T include all columns in correlation analysis without filtering — focus on numeric columns for stats
7. DON'T write the report until all visualizations are complete — collect results first, then generate final HTML
trigger_description: When you have raw CSV or JSON data files and need to automatically generate visualizations, identify patterns, and produce an embedded-image HTML report with insights.
reasoning: This pattern balances automated statistical discovery with LLM-guided insight generation, processes data sequentially to avoid memory bloat, handles script failures gracefully with retries, and produces a self-contained HTML report that requires no external file references.
tags: data-analysis, visualization, matplotlib, plotly, pandas, html-report, pattern-detection, batch-processing
examples: # Example workflow for a single CSV file: read(path='sales_data.csv') shell('python -c "import pandas as pd; df=pd.read_csv(\'sales_data.csv\'); print(df.describe()); print(df.corr())"') # LLM identifies: revenue trend over time, product category distribution, outlier transactions # Generate script for each: write(path='scripts/revenue_trend.py', content='import pandas as pd; import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; df=pd.read_csv(...); df.plot(x=\'date\', y=\'revenue\'); plt.savefig(\'revenue_trend.png\')') shell('python scripts/revenue_trend.py') image_vision(path='revenue_trend.png') write(path='insights/revenue_trend_insight.md', content='Chart shows 15% YoY growth...') # Repeat for other patterns, then: # Read all insight files, base64-encode all PNGs, generate final HTML with embedded images write(path='report.html', content='......')

name: Normalize Multi-Schema Data Files
do_pattern:
glob(pattern='<input_dir>/**/*.{csv,json}')
canonical_schema = infer from first valid file
mapping_log = []
FOR EACH file:
  TRY:
    data = read file (csv_extract or json parse)
    detected_schema = analyze columns & sample values
    inferred_mapping = match detected_schema to canonical_schema
    FOR EACH ambiguous column:
      IF confidence < threshold:
        ask user for confirmation
        update inferred_mapping
    normalized_data = apply mapping & format conversions
    append to output file
    log(file, mapping, conversions_applied)
  CATCH parse_error:
    log(file, 'SKIPPED', error_reason)
write(path='<output_dir>/normalized.csv', content=combined_data)
write(path='<output_dir>/mapping_log.json', content=mapping_log)
dont_pattern:
DON'T load all files into memory at once
DON'T assume column names match across files
DON'T apply format conversions without logging what changed
DON'T halt on first parse error — skip & continue
DON'T ask user to confirm every single column match
DON'T discard the original values — preserve them in the log
DON'T assume date/unit formats without heuristic confidence check
trigger_description: When you have multiple CSV/JSON files with the same logical data but inconsistent schemas (column names, date formats, units) and need a single normalized output.
reasoning: Normalizing multi-schema data requires inferring intent from samples, handling ambiguity gracefully with heuristics before asking, and logging transformations for auditability. Sequential per-file processing with immediate writes prevents memory bloat and allows the scale loop to manage large batches.
tags: data-transformation, schema-mapping, csv, json, format-conversion, batch-processing, error-handling
examples:
# Infer canonical schema from first file
data = read('input/sales_2024.csv')
schema = {col: infer_type(data[col]) for col in data.columns}

# Apply heuristic-based mapping
detected_cols = ['Date', 'Temp_C', 'Price_USD']
canonical_cols = ['date', 'temperature_celsius', 'price']
mapping = match_columns(detected_cols, canonical_cols, threshold=0.85)

# Log ambiguities for review
IF mapping_confidence < 0.7:
  ask_user(f"Map '{detected_col}' to which canonical field?")

# Write normalized output incrementally
FOR EACH file:
  normalized = transform(data, mapping)
  append_to_file('output/normalized.csv', normalized)
  log_mapping('output/mapping_log.json', file, mapping, conversions)

name: Mixed-Format Documents to Knowledge Base
do_pattern:
summarize_files(directory="<input_dir>", output="<summaries_dir>")
files = glob(pattern="<input_dir>/**/*")
FOR EACH file in files:
  IF file format unsupported OR extraction fails:
    log error to <output_dir>/errors.log
    continue
  content = extract via pdf_extract/docx_extract/read
  infer category from content via LLM
  extract title, topics from content
  write(path="<summaries_dir>/<slug>.md", content=summary)
read all summary files
group by inferred category
write(path="<output_dir>/index.md", content=toc + hierarchical sections + full index with links)
write(path="<output_dir>/errors.log", content=error_list)
dont_pattern:
DON'T load all documents into context at once
DON'T attempt extraction on unsupported formats without fallback
DON'T halt on first error — log and continue
DON'T infer categories in batch — process per-document
DON'T embed all summaries inline in index.md — link to individual pages
DON'T skip error logging — maintain errors.log for transparency
DON'T assume extraction always succeeds — wrap in try/catch logic
trigger_description: Convert a directory of mixed-format documents (PDF, DOCX, MD, TXT) into a structured knowledge base with categorized summaries and cross-referenced index.
reasoning: This pattern leverages summarize_files for efficient batch processing, processes documents sequentially to keep context bounded, infers categories per-document for accuracy, and maintains resilience via error logging — enabling knowledge base creation from heterogeneous sources without token explosion or data loss.
tags: document-processing, knowledge-base, batch-processing, mixed-formats, resilient, indexing
examples:
# Token-efficient workflow:
summarize_files(directory="./docs", output="./summaries")
files = glob(pattern="./docs/**/*.{pdf,docx,md,txt}")
FOR EACH file:
  TRY:
    IF file.ext == "pdf":
      content = pdf_extract(path=file)
    ELSE IF file.ext == "docx":
      content = docx_extract(path=file)
    ELSE:
      content = read(path=file)
    title, category, topics = LLM infer from content
    summary = extract key points
    write(path="./summaries/" + slug(title) + ".md", content=summary)
  CATCH error:
    append to errors_list

# Build index:
index_content = "# Knowledge Base\n\n## Table of Contents\n"
FOR EACH category in sorted(categories):
  index_content += "- [" + category + "](#" + slug(category) + ")\n"
index_content += "\n## By Category\n"
FOR EACH category in sorted(categories):
  index_content += "### " + category + "\n"
  FOR EACH doc in docs_in_category:
    index_content += "- [" + doc.title + "](./summaries/" + slug(doc.title) + ".md)\n"
index_content += "\n## Full Index\n"
FOR EACH doc in all_docs:
  index_content += "- [" + doc.title + "](./summaries/" + slug(doc.title) + ".md) — " + doc.category + "\n"

write(path="./output/index.md", content=index_content)
write(path="./output/errors.log", content=format_errors(errors_list))

name: Batch OCR with Filename Metadata Extraction
do_pattern:
files = glob(pattern="<input_dir>/**/*.{pdf,jpg,png,tiff}")
FOR EACH file in files:
  IF file is PDF:
    ocr_text = image_ocr(path=file)
  ELSE:
    ocr_text = image_ocr(path=file)
  clean_text = ocr_text  # no cleanup needed per user
  write(path="<output_dir>/<basename>.txt", content=clean_text)
  parsed = parse_filename(file)  # extract date and title
  index_entry = {filename, title: parsed.title, date: parsed.date, output_file}
  append to index_list
write(path="<output_dir>/index.md", content=format_index(index_list))
dont_pattern:
# WRONG: collect all OCR results in memory before writing
ocr_results = []
for file in files:
  ocr_results.append(image_ocr(file))
# now process all results (context explodes)

# WRONG: try to extract title/date from OCR text via pattern matching
for file in files:
  text = image_ocr(file)
  title = extract_title_from_text(text)  # fragile, unreliable
  date = extract_date_from_text(text)    # fragile, unreliable

# WRONG: process all files at once without per-file writes
all_text = ""
for file in files:
  all_text += image_ocr(file) + "\n---\n"
write(path="output.txt", content=all_text)  # single large file
trigger_description: Process a directory of scanned images or PDFs with OCR, extract text, and generate an index with titles and dates parsed from filenames.
reasoning: Processing files sequentially with immediate per-file writes leverages the scale loop's context trimming, preventing memory bloat. Extracting metadata from filenames is reliable and avoids fragile OCR-based pattern matching. A structured index enables downstream workflows to reference processed documents.
tags: ocr, batch-processing, document-processing, file-management, metadata-extraction, scale-loop
examples:
# Filename format: Invoice-ABC-2024-01-15.pdf
# Parsing logic:
filename = "Invoice-ABC-2024-01-15.pdf"
parts = filename.split('-')
title = '-'.join(parts[:-1])  # "Invoice-ABC"
date = parts[-1].replace('.pdf', '')  # "2024-01-15"

# Index output (index.md):
| Filename | Title | Date | Output File |
|----------|-------|------|-------------|
| Invoice-ABC-2024-01-15.pdf | Invoice-ABC | 2024-01-15 | Invoice-ABC-2024-01-15.txt |
| Receipt-XYZ-2024-02-20.jpg | Receipt-XYZ | 2024-02-20 | Receipt-XYZ-2024-02-20.txt |

name: Batch Markdown Normalization
do_pattern:
files = glob(pattern="<input_dir>/**/*.md")
FOR EACH file in files:
  content = read(path=file)
  extract title from first H1 or filename
  fix heading hierarchy (ensure H1 exists, demote orphaned H2+)
  add/update frontmatter with title, date (blank if missing), tags (auto from headings)
  FOR EACH link in content:
    validate link target exists or normalize path
    convert absolute → relative where applicable
    fix extension mismatches (.markdown → .md)
  normalized = frontmatter + fixed_content
  write(path=file, content=normalized)
  # scale loop auto-trims read content after write
trigger_description: Normalize a directory of markdown files to standardized format with consistent headings, frontmatter, and link validation.
reasoning: Sequential per-file processing with immediate write allows the scale loop to trim context after each file, keeping memory constant regardless of directory size. Frontmatter auto-extraction and link validation ensure consistency without manual intervention.
tags: markdown, batch-processing, normalization, frontmatter, link-validation, file-management
examples:
# Pseudo-code for one file iteration:
file = "docs/guide.md"
content = read(path=file)

# Extract title from first H1
title = extract_first_h1(content) or filename_to_title(file)

# Fix heading hierarchy
content = fix_heading_hierarchy(content)

# Build frontmatter
frontmatter = "---\ntitle: " + title + "\ndate: \ntags: [" + auto_tags(content) + "]\n---\n"

# Fix links
for link in find_links(content):
  if is_absolute(link):
    link = make_relative(link, file)
  if has_wrong_extension(link):
    link = normalize_extension(link)
  if not file_exists(resolve_path(link)):
    link = find_similar_file(link)  # or mark as broken
  content = replace_link(content, old_link, link)

# Write back
write(path=file, content=frontmatter + content)

name: Bulk Rename Files by Content
do_pattern:
files = glob(pattern="<input_dir>/**/*.(pdf|docx|jpg|png|jpeg)")
rename_log = []
FOR EACH file in files:
  metadata = extract_metadata(file)  # date, type
  content = extract_content(file)    # pdf_extract, docx_extract, image_ocr, image_vision
  IF metadata.date exists:
    suggested_name = format_name(date=metadata.date, topic=infer_topic(content), type=file_type)
  ELSE:
    suggested_name = infer_from_llm(content, file_type)
  IF suggested_name is confident:
    new_path = <input_dir>/<suggested_name>
    shell(mv <old_path> <new_path>)
    rename_log.append({old: basename(file), new: basename(new_path), status: "renamed"})
  ELSE:
    rename_log.append({old: basename(file), new: "N/A", status: "flagged_for_review"})
write(path="<output_dir>/rename_log.csv", content=csv_format(rename_log))
dont_pattern:
# WRONG: collect all files in memory, then process
all_files = []
for file in glob(...):
  all_files.append(file)
for file in all_files:  # context explodes
  process(file)

# WRONG: extract all content upfront before renaming
contents = {}
for file in files:
  contents[file] = extract_content(file)
for file, content in contents.items():  # stale context
  rename(file, content)

# WRONG: generate all mv commands, then execute batch
commands = []
for file in files:
  commands.append(f"mv {old} {new}")
shell("\n".join(commands))  # no per-file error handling
trigger_description: Rename a directory of PDFs, images, and DOCX files based on extracted content metadata and LLM analysis, logging all changes to CSV.
reasoning: Processing files one-at-a-time with immediate rename + log write leverages the scale loop's context trimming, avoiding memory bloat. Metadata-first + LLM-fallback + manual review flag ensures high-quality renames without losing ambiguous files. CSV log provides audit trail for verification.
tags: file-management, bulk-rename, content-extraction, metadata, logging, scale-loop
examples:
# Extract metadata from PDF
content = pdf_extract(path="invoice_2024.pdf")
metadata = {date: "2024-01-15", type: "pdf"}

# Extract from image with OCR + vision
ocr_text = image_ocr(path="photo.jpg")
vision = image_vision(path="photo.jpg", prompt="What is the main subject?")

# Extract from DOCX
content = docx_extract(path="report.docx")

# LLM-suggested name (if metadata insufficient)
# Send: {extracted_text, file_type} → Claude → {suggested_name, confidence}

# Execute rename
shell(mv "old_invoice.pdf" "2024-01-15_invoice_financial.pdf")

# Log entry
old_name,new_name,status
invoice_2024.pdf,2024-01-15_invoice_financial.pdf,renamed
photo_unknown.jpg,N/A,flagged_for_review

name: In-Depth News Event Research & Briefing
do_pattern:
web_search(query="<headline or topic>", num_results=10)
FOR EACH result in top 10:
  content = web_fetch(url=result.url)
  IF content is empty or incomplete:
    note_unavailable(url=result.url, reason="incomplete or paywalled")
    CONTINUE
  extracted = LLM_extract(content, fields=["facts", "quotes", "timeline_events", "perspectives"])
  summary = LLM_synthesize(extracted, format="per-source summary")
  write(path="research/<source_slug>_summary.md", content=summary)
  # scale loop auto-trims fetched content here
read all summaries from research/
COMPILE timeline_events from all sources
COMPILE fact_checks by cross-referencing claims across sources
SYNTHESIZE comprehensive briefing with sections:
  - Executive summary
  - Timeline (chronological events)
  - Key facts & quotes (by source)
  - Different perspectives (grouped by viewpoint)
  - Fact-check notes (contradictions, unverified claims)
  - Source availability notes
write(path="<output_file>.md", content=briefing)
dont_pattern:
DON'T fetch all 10 articles into context at once before processing
DON'T attempt to extract facts from paywalled content without browser automation
DON'T skip unavailable sources silently — always log them
DON'T assume all sources will be available — handle gracefully
DON'T store extracted data in memory — write per-source summaries immediately
DON'T mix raw article text with synthesis — separate extraction from final briefing
DON'T fact-check in isolation — cross-reference claims across all sources first
DON'T produce output until all available sources are processed
trigger_description: When you need comprehensive coverage of a news event or topic with extracted facts, quotes, timeline, and cross-source synthesis into a single briefing document.
reasoning: This pattern ensures token efficiency by processing articles one-at-a-time with immediate summarization (leveraging the scale loop's context trimming), handles unavailable sources gracefully, and produces a single authoritative briefing that synthesizes facts, timelines, and perspectives across all sources with explicit fact-checking.
tags: web-research, news-analysis, fact-checking, synthesis, timeline-extraction, multi-source, scale-loop
examples:
web_search(query="2024 election debate analysis")
web_fetch(url="https://example.com/article1")
# Returns HTML → convert to markdown
extracted = {"facts": [...], "quotes": [...], "timeline": [...], "perspectives": [...]}
write(path="research/source_a_summary.md", content="## Source A Summary\n...")
# Repeat for sources 2-10
# Then compile:
write(path="briefing.md", content="# Comprehensive Briefing: 2024 Election Debate\n## Timeline\n- [date] Event 1\n- [date] Event 2\n## Fact-Check Notes\n- Claim X: Verified by sources A, B; disputed by C\n...")

name: Technology Comparison Report Builder
do_pattern:
web_search(query="<tech> official documentation") + web_search(query="<tech> benchmarks performance") + web_search(query="<tech> reviews pros cons")
FOR EACH technology:
  FOR EACH search result (top 3-5):
    content = web_fetch(url=result.url)
    extract: performance metrics, features, cost, maturity, pros, cons, use cases
    prioritize official docs > benchmarks > reviews by authority
    write(path="research/<tech>_findings.md", content=extracted_data)
    # scale loop auto-trims fetched content
read all research/<tech>_findings.md files
BUILD comparison matrix:
  rows = [Performance, Features, Cost, Maturity, Community]
  cols = [<tech1>, <tech2>, <tech3>, ...]
  FOR EACH cell: score 1-5 or mark N/A if missing
  resolve conflicts by picking most authoritative source
write(path="comparison_report.md", content=matrix + recommendation_summary)
dont_pattern:
DON'T fetch all URLs upfront and load into context
DON'T collect all findings in memory before writing
DON'T average conflicting scores without noting source authority
DON'T skip criteria entirely if one tech lacks data — use N/A
DON'T mix official docs with random blog posts without ranking authority
DON'T produce the matrix before extracting all tech data
DON'T include raw HTML or unstructured web content in final report
trigger_description: When you need to compare 3-5 technologies across performance, features, cost, and maturity to produce a scored side-by-side matrix and recommendation.
reasoning: This pattern leverages sequential fetch-extract-write to keep context constant while building a comprehensive, scored comparison. Prioritizing source authority prevents conflicting data from skewing results, and N/A markers preserve transparency when data is unavailable.
tags: web-research, comparison, benchmarking, technology-evaluation, matrix-generation, multi-source-synthesis
examples:
web_search(query="React official documentation")
web_search(query="React performance benchmarks 2024")
web_search(query="React vs Vue pros cons reviews")

FOR tech in [React, Vue, Svelte]:
  FOR url in top_results:
    content = web_fetch(url=url)
    extract {performance_score, bundle_size, learning_curve, ecosystem_size, ...}
    write(path="research/React_findings.md", content=findings)

Final matrix (markdown table):
| Criterion | React | Vue | Svelte |
|-----------|-------|-----|--------|
| Performance (1-5) | 4 | 4 | 5 |
| Learning Curve (1-5) | 3 | 4 | 4 |
| Ecosystem (1-5) | 5 | 3 | 2 |
| Cost | Free | Free | Free |
| Maturity (1-5) | 5 | 4 | 3 |
| Recommendation | Enterprise | Mid-size | Startups |

name: Job Market Trend Research & Report
do_pattern:
web_search(query="<role> salary survey 2024")
web_search(query="<role> job market trends")
web_search(query="<role> required skills demand")
COMBINE top 8 results across all searches
FOR EACH result (one at a time):
  content = web_fetch(url=result.url)
  EXTRACT: salary_range, top_skills, demand_level, remote_ratio, market_insights
  write(path="research/<source_slug>.md", content=markdown_summary)
  # scale loop auto-trims fetched content
read all summaries from research/
AGGREGATE: salary ranges, skill frequencies, demand consensus, work arrangement patterns
GENERATE markdown report with:
  - Executive summary
  - Salary trends (table + analysis)
  - Top skills (frequency-ranked)
  - Market demand assessment
  - Remote vs onsite ratios
  - Sources cited
write(path="job_market_report.md", content=final_report)
dont_pattern:
DON'T fetch all 8 URLs into context at once before processing
DON'T store extracted data in memory — write each summary immediately
DON'T skip source attribution or conflicting data points
DON'T use generic web_search queries — be specific to role + data type
DON'T aggregate without tracking which source said what
DON'T produce report without salary/skills/demand/arrangement data
DON'T ignore outlier data — flag it in the report
trigger_description: When you need to analyze salary surveys, job postings, and market reports for a specific role to produce an aggregated job market report with trends.
reasoning: Sequential fetch-summarize-write leverages the scale loop's context trimming, preventing token explosion while capturing rich market data. Markdown summaries enable easy aggregation and final report generation without losing source attribution.
tags: web-research, job-market, salary-analysis, skills-assessment, market-trends, data-aggregation, report-generation
examples:
web_search(query="Senior Data Engineer salary survey 2024")
web_search(query="Data Engineer job market trends remote")
web_search(query="Data Engineer required skills demand analysis")

For each fetched page:
content = web_fetch(url="https://example.com/salary-report")
Extract and write:
```markdown
## Source: Example Salary Report
- **Salary Range**: $120k–$180k (US, Senior level)
- **Top Skills**: Python, SQL, Spark, Kubernetes, dbt
- **Demand Level**: High (15% YoY growth)
- **Remote Ratio**: 60% fully remote, 30% hybrid, 10% onsite
- **Key Insight**: Cloud data platforms (Snowflake, BigQuery) increasingly required
```

Final report structure:
```markdown
# Job Market Report: Senior Data Engineer

## Executive Summary
[1-2 paragraph synthesis]

## Salary Trends
| Level | Low | Mid | High | Source Count |
|-------|-----|-----|------|---------------|
| Senior | $120k | $155k | $190k | 5 |

## Top In-Demand Skills
1. Python (8/8 sources)
2. SQL (8/8 sources)
3. Spark (7/8 sources)
...

## Market Demand
[Growth rates, hiring trends]

## Work Arrangements
- Fully Remote: 60%
- Hybrid: 30%
- Onsite: 10%

## Sources
[Numbered list with URLs]
```

name: Regulatory Change Tracking & Reporting
do_pattern:
web_search(query="<industry> regulatory changes <year>", date_filter="recent")
FOR EACH of top 8 results:
  content = web_fetch(url=result.url)
  extract: regulation_name, agency, effective_date, impact_summary
  filename = "regulations/<agency>_<regulation-name>_<YYYY-MM-DD>.md"
  write(path=filename, content="# <regulation_name>\n\nAgency: <agency>\nEffective Date: <effective_date>\n\n<impact_summary>")
  # scale loop auto-trims fetched content
files = glob(pattern="regulations/*.md")
sort files by effective_date (extracted from filename)
read all sorted files
write(path="regulatory_update_report.md", content="# Regulatory Update Report\n\n<chronologically ordered regulations>")
dont_pattern:
# WRONG: fetch all 8 URLs into context first, then process
all_content = []
FOR EACH of top 8 results:
  all_content.append(web_fetch(url=result.url))
# now extract from all_content (context explodes)

# WRONG: store effective dates in memory, sort in-context
effective_dates = []
FOR EACH result:
  extract date, append to list
sort(effective_dates) in Python/JS

# WRONG: write single combined file during loop
FOR EACH result:
  append to regulatory_update_report.md (inefficient, repeated reads/writes)
trigger_description: When you need to monitor and document recent regulatory updates in a specific industry, with structured extraction and chronological reporting.
reasoning: Sequential fetch-extract-write with immediate file persistence leverages the scale loop's context trimming, preventing token explosion when processing 8+ web pages. Filename-based sorting avoids in-memory date collection and enables reliable chronological ordering via glob + filename parsing.
tags: web-research, regulatory-monitoring, batch-processing, document-extraction, date-sorting, scale-loop-optimized
examples:
# Example web_search query:
web_search(query="healthcare HIPAA regulatory changes 2024")

# Example per-regulation file (regulations/HHS_HIPAA-Breach-Notification_2024-03-15.md):
# HIPAA Breach Notification Rule Update

Agency: HHS (Department of Health and Human Services)
Effective Date: 2024-03-15

## Impact Summary
Expands definition of unsecured PHI, requires notification within 30 days of discovery, applies to business associates and subcontractors.

# Example final report structure:
# Regulatory Update Report

## 2024-03-15: HIPAA Breach Notification Rule Update
- Agency: HHS
- Impact: Expands definition of unsecured PHI...

## 2024-05-20: SEC Climate Disclosure Rule
- Agency: SEC
- Impact: Public companies must disclose climate risks...

name: Academic Paper Research & Literature Review
do_pattern:
web_search(query="<topic> academic paper research")
FOR EACH of top 10 results:
  content = web_fetch(url=result.url)
  IF content contains PDF link:
    pdf_content = web_fetch(url=pdf_link) # attempt PDF extraction
  ELSE:
    pdf_content = null
  extract title, authors, abstract, key_findings from (pdf_content OR content)
  relevance_score = assess_match_to_<topic>(0-100)
  write(path="papers/<slug>_summary.md", content=formatted_summary)
  # scale loop auto-trims fetched content
read all summary files from papers/
identify_topic_subtopics(summaries) → group papers by theme
rank_by_relevance_within_each_theme()
write(path="literature_review.md", content=review_with_table_and_themes)
dont_pattern:
# WRONG: fetch all papers into context at once
all_papers = []
for url in search_results:
  all_papers.append(web_fetch(url))
# context explodes, scale loop can't trim

# WRONG: skip writing summaries, try to synthesize from memory
for url in urls:
  content = web_fetch(url)
  # attempt to hold all content in context

# WRONG: ignore PDF links, only parse HTML
# misses full-text content and key findings

# WRONG: organize review only by publication date
# loses thematic structure and relevance ranking
trigger_description: When you need to find and synthesize 5+ academic papers on a topic into a structured literature review with thematic organization.
reasoning: Academic research requires synthesizing multiple sources efficiently without exploding context. Fetching and writing per-paper summaries one-at-a-time lets the scale loop trim fetched content, while organizing by theme + relevance ensures the final review is both discoverable and actionable.
tags: web-research, academic, literature-review, batch-processing, pdf-extraction, thematic-synthesis
examples:
# Search and fetch loop (token-efficient)
web_search(query="transformer models NLP")
# Returns: [arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762, openreview.net/pdf?id=..., ...]

FOR EACH url in top_10:
  html = web_fetch(url=url)
  # If arxiv: extract PDF link from html
  if 'arxiv.org' in url:
    pdf_url = extract_pdf_link(html)  # e.g., arxiv.org/pdf/1706.03762.pdf
    pdf_text = web_fetch(url=pdf_url)
  else:
    pdf_text = null
  
  title = extract_title(pdf_text or html)
  authors = extract_authors(pdf_text or html)
  abstract = extract_abstract(pdf_text or html)
  findings = extract_key_findings(pdf_text or html)
  relevance = score_relevance(title + abstract, query="transformer models NLP")
  
  summary = format_as_markdown(title, authors, abstract, findings, relevance)
  write(path="papers/arxiv_1706_03762.md", content=summary)

# Final synthesis
summaries = read(path="papers/")  # read all .md files
themes = identify_themes(summaries)  # e.g., ["Attention Mechanisms", "Training Efficiency", "Scaling Laws"]

FOR EACH theme:
  papers_in_theme = filter_by_theme(summaries, theme)
  ranked = sort_by_relevance(papers_in_theme)
  add_section_to_review(theme, ranked)

write(path="literature_review.md", content=review_with_summary_table)

name: Competitor Pricing Monitor with Change Detection
do_pattern:
web_search(query="<competitor> pricing") [optional: if URLs not provided]
FOR EACH competitor_url in <url_list>:
  page = web_fetch(url=competitor_url)
  extract pricing_tiers = [name, price, currency, billing_period, features]
  write(path="pricing/<competitor>_current.md", content=markdown_table(pricing_tiers))
  FOR EACH tier in pricing_tiers:
    old_tier = datastore.query(table="competitor_pricing", where={competitor, tier_name})
    IF old_tier exists:
      price_change_pct = abs((tier.price - old_tier.price) / old_tier.price) * 100
      feature_diff = compare_feature_lists(tier.features, old_tier.features)
      IF price_change_pct > 5 OR price_change_abs > 10 OR feature_diff:
        flag_change(competitor, tier, change_type, magnitude)
    datastore.upsert(table="competitor_pricing", record={competitor, tier_name, price, currency, billing_period, features, timestamp})
read all flagged_changes
write(path="pricing_report.md", content=comparison_matrix(flagged_changes_only))
dont_pattern:
# WRONG: fetch all URLs into context first
all_pages = [web_fetch(url) for url in url_list]
process all_pages at once

# WRONG: store pricing in memory, compare at end
pricing_dict = {}
for url in url_list:
  pricing_dict[url] = extract_pricing(web_fetch(url))
compare_all_at_once(pricing_dict)

# WRONG: no historical tracking
write current pricing to file
manually diff against old file

# WRONG: flag every tiny change
if price_changed_at_all:
  flag_as_change

# WRONG: include unchanged competitors in final report
matrix = all_competitors
highlight changes in matrix
trigger_description: Monitor competitor pricing pages for changes, extract structured pricing data, store historically, and flag significant price movements or feature changes.
reasoning: Sequential fetch-extract-store-compare prevents context explosion, datastore enables historical tracking and change detection without manual diffing, and threshold-based flagging reduces noise while capturing material pricing shifts.
tags: competitor-intelligence, pricing-monitoring, web-research, datastore, change-detection, batch-processing
examples:
# Fetch and extract one competitor
page = web_fetch(url="https://example.com/pricing")
# Extract into structured format:
# | Tier | Price | Billing | Features |
# | Basic | $29 | /month | Feature A, Feature B |
# | Pro | $99 | /month | Feature A, B, C, D |

# Store in datastore
datastore.insert(table="competitor_pricing", record={
  competitor: "example.com",
  tier_name: "Pro",
  price: 99,
  currency: "USD",
  billing_period: "month",
  features: ["Feature A", "Feature B", "Feature C", "Feature D"],
  timestamp: "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z"
})

# Query historical data
old_record = datastore.query(table="competitor_pricing", where={competitor: "example.com", tier_name: "Pro"})
# Compare: if old_record.price was 89, new is 99 → 11.2% increase → FLAG

# Final output: pricing_report.md contains only flagged changes
# | Competitor | Tier | Old Price | New Price | Change % | Features Added/Removed |
# | example.com | Pro | $89 | $99 | +11.2% | Added: Feature D |

name: Pre-Sales Company Research Brief
do_pattern:
web_search(query="<company_name> recent news 2024")
FOR EACH result in top 5:
  content = web_fetch(url=result.url)
  extract dates, names, financial figures
  write(path="research/<company>_news_<index>.md", content=findings)
REPEAT for: "<company_name> funding rounds", "<company_name> key executives", "<company_name> product launches"
read all research/*.md files
write(path="company-brief.md", content={
  Overview: <company_name> + domain,
  Recent News: synthesize news findings,
  Key People: synthesize executive findings,
  Products: synthesize product findings,
  Funding: synthesize funding findings,
  Confidence Notes: flag low-confidence results with disclaimers
})
dont_pattern:
DON'T fetch all URLs upfront and collect in memory
DON'T run a single generic search for all four topics
DON'T discard pages with partial/outdated info — extract and flag
DON'T skip low-relevance results without noting them
DON'T combine findings into a single file during research loop
DON'T assume all four categories will have equal coverage
trigger_description: When preparing for a sales call and need a comprehensive company overview including recent news, funding, key people, and products.
reasoning: Sequential fetch-extract-write per result leverages the scale loop's context trimming, keeping memory constant regardless of search volume. Separate searches per category ensure targeted, relevant results. Flagging low-confidence findings preserves data integrity while maintaining transparency for sales prep.
tags: web-research, sales-prep, company-intelligence, batch-processing, markdown-output
examples:
web_search(query="Acme Corp recent news 2024")
web_fetch(url="https://techcrunch.com/acme-series-b")
extract: "Series B funding, $50M, led by Sequoia, Q3 2024"
write(path="research/acme_funding_1.md", content="**Acme Corp Series B Funding**\n- Amount: $50M\n- Lead: Sequoia Capital\n- Date: Q3 2024\n- Source: TechCrunch")

Final output structure:
# Acme Corp Company Brief
## Overview
Acme Corp (acme.com) — AI-powered supply chain platform
## Recent News
[synthesized from news_*.md]
## Key People
[synthesized from executives_*.md]
## Products
[synthesized from products_*.md]
## Funding
[synthesized from funding_*.md]
## Confidence Notes
⚠️ Result #3 from news search was low-confidence (outdated 2022 article) — included with disclaimer
